---
title-block-banner: true
title: "An Intro to the Data Science Tools"
subtitle: "To Ellinor"
author: "Phillip Post"
date: "04/21/2026"
toc: True
toc-expand: 1
format: live-html
---

{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

::: callout-important
## No Artificial Intelligence (AI) was used to produce this. All errors are attributable to Phillip.
:::

# **Why does this exist?**

The goal of this piece is to allow the reader to triage what programming language, setup, tools, and processes would be best for them to answer novel questions. There are a bevy of excellent, free resources online that a person, no matter their education or background, can use to match the leading researchers and institutions. Paradoxically the vast number of these resources can lead to an information overload and paralysis, with a new or experienced researcher unsure of what path to take since they are so overwhelmed with the seemingly infinite possibilities.

While admittedly adding to this noise, this guide offers the resources that I have found the most compelling in my research career, which means they may or may not be useful for yourself. Academia is a field guilty of arbitrary gatekeeping and litmus tests against someone who simply has the curiosity to ask why, but this does a fundamental disservice to us all. Compelling research does not result from an academic title, prestigious institution, or specific place, it comes from someone who has the willingness to learn, share their unique perspectives, and have an equal review and conversation with their peers.

Since I equally love R & Python, I will discuss everything in this article through the process of doing things in either language. Even if you only use one, *which you probably will*, it doesn't hurt to be familiar with what else is out there.

All of the code cells in this notebook are editable and customizable, so please feel free to play around with them! You can execute code by clicking the `Run Code` button and can always undo your changes by clicking the `Start Over` button.

For example a python cell:

```{pyodide}
# create a variable named x that stores the integer value 2
x = 2

# add x to 3 but don't assign the value to any location, implicitly printing it
x + 3
```

And now an R cell:

```{webr}
# create a variable named x that stores the integer value 2
x = 2

# add x to 3 but don't assign the value to any location, implicitly printing it
x + 3
```

# **The Data Science Workflow**

In my mind, the general tasks a data scientist performs from the inception to completion of a research project is as follows,

+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Data Acquisition 🔍                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | Data Processing 🚧                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          | Data Storytelling 🎥                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
+::+:===========================================================================================================================================================================================================================================================================================================:+::+
| This the task of finding, scraping (if the data is not already structured), and loading a dataset. There are plenty of publicly accessible datasets you can export as a `csv` or other data type and load directly, but this data is commonly already analyzed or published as it's the easiest to access. However, this in no way means the data is less relevant or valuable, it just means the author will have to combine it with more datasets or use a novel `Data Storytelling` method to add to contemporary literature. | Data processing or data wrangling is the process of cleaning, manipulating, and fundamentally changing the data you have from `Data Acquisition`.                                                                                                                                                           | Whereas `Data Acquisition` and `Data Processing` would be the methods of a typical research paper, `Data Storytelling` is your conclusion and results. It is your opportunity to share with the world the magnum opus of your work, learning, and knowledge. Whether this takes the form of timelines, figures, or tables (usually a combination of all three), they should all contribute to a compelling narrative that anyone could follow, even if they are not a peer in your field. |
|                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           |
| If you already have a dataset that you want to analyze you only have to pay attention to the data loading part of this section.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | This could mean something as simple summing a column of cocoa bean costs to find the total amount, or something as complex having three LLMs collectively read an unstructured text and vote on whether they believe the sentiment of the text is negative, neutral, or positive. The world is your oyster. | Chefs pour their soul into a meal, artists their art piece, and a data scientist into their figures.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      |
+----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------+

# **R 🟦 vs Python 🐍**

R and Python shouldn't be thought of as competitors but rather complimentary programming languages that have their own strengths and weaknesses. I have had many projects where I use Python and R. For example, I may use Python to pull and download datasets from a website, but then use R for the data processing and visuals.

While they have their own pros and cons, you should use the language that makes the most sense for you and critically the one that **you can hold yourself accountable to consistently use.** The best programming language at the end of the day is the language you can program in. This section will run through the most fundamental differences in syntax/workflows between these two languages.

But at a high level the pros and cons of each is

## Loading Packages 🕛

Note that this does not involved the download process, which is covered in @sec-setup.

### For Python

For Python you use import to load a package:

```{pyodide}
import os
```

Python comes with many helpful packages like `os` preinstalled within the language itself, so you don't have to separately install them as an external package.

In Python, the `.` operator allows you to load dependent functions, scripts, or objects located within a package, which is basically just a python file. So if I want to access the very useful `join` function in the `os` package to join together a file path, I can do so:

```{pyodide}
# note, you need os loaded to access it so to run this cell, you have to have run the prior import os cell
os.path.join("random", "path", "to", "file.txt")
```

But its a pain to call `os.path.join` every single time so instead we can give it another shorthand name using the `from` and `as` arguments:

```{pyodide}
from os.path import join as here

here("random", "path", "to", "file.txt")
```

### For R

For R you use the `library` function to load a package. Unlike Python where you have to be very specific and explicitly load a function or use `.` to access a specific function within a package, R loads everything within a package at once. Because of that, all of R's default packages and their functions are already loaded and we can reference them directly:

```{webr}
file.path("random", "path", "to", "file.txt")
```

Notice I didn't use the `library` function? This is because file.path is a default R function, and we only have to load external packages, which will be covered in @sec-setup.

## Fundamental Data Types 📁

### Decimal values

Floats in Python

```{pyodide}
x = 3.1
type(x)
```

numeric/double in R

```{webr}
x = 3.1
class(x)
```

### Integer Values

Python has a separate class for integers

```{pyodide}
x = 3
type(x)
```

R considers integers and floats to be numeric

```{webr}
x = 3
class(x)
```

### Text data

String in python

```{pyodide}
x = "bean"
type(x)
```

Character in R

```{webr}
x = "bean"
class(x)
```

### Vectors vs Lists

Python lists allow any object of any type, including more lists for added dimensions

```{pyodide}
x = [5, 3.1, "bean", True]
type(x)
```

R uses vectors which only allow a single data type and are one dimensional, notice how a vector is the same type as the data it contains

```{webr}
x = c(1,2,3,4)
class(x)
```

### Booleans

Bool in python

```{pyodide}
x = True
type(x)
```

Logical in R

```{webr}
x = TRUE
class(x)
```

```{webr}
x = T
class(x)
```

### if else

if else in Python, uses tab spacing to indicate what is within what

```{pyodide}
if True:
    print("well this is surprising")
else:
    print(":O")

```

But R uses parantheses and curly brackets with no regard for spacing

```{webr}
    x = T
if (x){
    print("very surprising")
} else{
print(":O")
}
```

### and

Python uses and

```{pyodide}
if True and False:
    print("well this is surprising")
else:
    print(":O")

```

R uses &

```{webr}
    x = T
y = F
if (x & y){
    print("very surprising")
} else{
print(":O")
}

```

### or

Python uses or

```{pyodide}
if True or False:
    print("well this is surprising")
else:
    print(":O")

```

R uses \|

```{webr}
    x = T
y = F
if (x | y){
    print("very surprising")
} else{
print(":O")
}

```

### not

Python uses not

```{pyodide}
if not True:
    print("well this is surprising")
else:
    print(":O")

```

R uses !

```{webr}
    x = T
if (!x){
    print("very surprising")
} else{
print(":O")
}

```

### What about dataframes, datatables, etc?

Python doesn't have a native way to handle this data but we will discuss packages in @sec-acq/

R does have a native `data.frame` object but it isn't amazing without some additional packages as well.

# **Data Acquisition 🔍** {#sec-acq}

This is the workflow where Python is far superior. Most public APIs have a python native version, making data acquisition and analysis a breeze.

On this note some notable Python packages to be aware of are:

## Requests

<https://pypi.org/project/requests/>

Allows you to make HTTP requests

## Polars

<https://pypi.org/project/polars/>

Can read online CSV or JSON files directly as a dataframe you can manipulate.

# **Data Processing 🚧**

This is where Python and R are evenly matched in terms of packages and capabilities. Although, polars is much faster and efficient than dplyr.

## For Python

### Polars

<https://pypi.org/project/polars/>

The best package for processing large datasets from my experience.

### statsmodels

<https://pypi.org/project/statsmodels/>

Widely used package for statistical models and tests, but Polars will cover most of your needs.

## For R

### tidyverse - dplyr

<https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/>

The tidyverse is one mega package within R that contains almost everything you need to perform analysis start to finish, specifically for manipulating datasets the dplyr package is used. R also is nice because it has statistical models like linear regressions built within it.

# **Data Storytelling 🎥**

Again, both Python and R are evenly matched in terms of packages and capabilities

## For Python

### plotly

<https://plotly.com/python/>

Nice interactive plots and maps, can also be saved as static images.

### plotnine

<https://plotnine.org/>

Great static plotting library that uses the same syntax as R's ggplot library.

## For R

### ggplot2

<https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/>

One of the most famous plotting packages with options to make almost any graph imaginable with lots of customizability. Note that this package is being ported to native Python through the plotnine package.

# **Computer 💻 or Cloud ☁️ Setup** {#sec-setup}

Before reading this section, please keep in mind that as with the rest of this article, no one approach is outright superior or better. Try and use the one that proves to have the least amount of friction for your brain and unique workflows.

The most fundamental difference between programming in R versus Python is the environment setup process, with Python being harder than R to setup but has more community built capabilities. Every programming language revolves around the interpreter (i.e. the low level code that parses the text we call Python or R and translates it into machine level code).

One more thing to keep in mind is the unsung hero of programming is good folder management, meaning you create folders with good but brief names, keep files in their proper folders, and documenting things so future you or a colleague can understand your choices.

## Computer 💻

The benefit of going down this route is that you will be able to theoretically run any package locally without an internet connection, unless the code itself requires one. The major downside of this route is you have to maintain your environments and keep things up to date. Also keep in mind code that works on your machine will probably not automatically work on someone else's, but there are ways to standardize your code to run on any computer (just requires more work).

Note this requires use of your terminal, which is your way of directly interacting with your computer's operating system.

Also note that you are at risk of losing your work if your computer dies, so make sure to do routine backups with an online data storage service. My recommendation is the dedicated free code storage tool named Github: <https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/start-your-journey/about-github-and-git>

### For Python & R

You need an interactive developer environment (IDE) to efficiently interact with these languages. My personal recommendation is Positron since it has lots of quality life features for performing data analysis.

Positron download: <https://positron.posit.co/download.html>

For interactive python programming you can use jupyter notebooks, which have the ipynb file extension. Marimo and Quarto provide good alternatives as well.

### For Python

The hardest part of programming in Python is package version control and management. Thankfully there is software that does this for us!

Install Anaconda for your computer: <https://www.anaconda.com/download/success?reg=skipped>

After you install and successfully set this up, you should be able to run the following commands **in your terminal** after restarting it:

To check that Anaconda is installed `conda version`

Create an environment, think of this as a self contained version of python and packages that exist for a specific task or project. You can use one environment for everything, but this will typically lead to bugs and issues overtime. `conda create -n example`

And then to enter this environment you would run `conda activate example`

You should see the environment name in your terminal

And now that we are within the environment, **we can install packages** `pip install polars`

And if it says pip is not installed you can run `anaconda install pip`

Lastly, if you want to create an interactive python notebook, create a file in your IDE with the extension .ipynb, this is the same file type that google collab uses.

### For R

You can also use Anaconda to ensure you do not have package conflicts but it is less pressing than Python. My personal recommendation is to at least use the pacman package in R since this streamlines package updates and install.

```{code}
# install pacman if it is not already installed
if (!"pacman" %in% installed.packages()){
install.packages("pacman")
}

# load pacman
library(pacman)

# load every other package with pacman, which will handle install and loading
pacman::p_load("here")
```

*If you do not use Anaconda*, you will have to install the R language here: <https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/base/>

## Cloud ☁️

For this, I personally recommend google collab, which allows you to easily share and store your code. Further, being in the cloud means your data is always saved. However, storage in google collab is not persistent so you will have to link your google drive for persistent storage.

### For Python

Example notebook for google collab in python: <https://colab.research.google.com/github/cs231n/cs231n.github.io/blob/master/python-colab.ipynb>

Note to install a python package in google collab you run `!pip install polars` in one of the cells

### For R

Example notebook for google collab in R: <https://colab.research.google.com/github/alvinntnu/python-notes/blob/master/python-basics/google-colab-r.ipynb>
